patches.find:
Klaus Meyl, a German electrical engineer, published experiments in the 1990s–2000s claiming that his coil apparatus transmitted energy through a fully enclosed Faraday cage via 'scalar waves' — a proposed longitudinal electromagnetic mode absent from Maxwell's equations. These results appeared exclusively in non-indexed conference proceedings and self-published volumes; no peer-reviewed journal has published Meyl's Faraday penetration claims.

The kernel of truth: near-field coupling is real. At distances shorter than λ/2π from the source (the reactive near-field zone), electromagnetic fields have large non-propagating evanescent components that can couple energy through conductive enclosures via cable shield capacitance, finite seam conductivity, and magnetic induction through apertures. Meyl's apparatus operates at MHz frequencies (λ ≈ 30–300 m), placing his coil separations of centimetres to metres deep inside the near-field zone where λ/2π ≈ 5–50 m. In this regime, energy transfer through a Faraday cage is expected from standard antenna theory — it is the same mechanism exploited by Qi wireless charging, NFC, and resonant inductive coupling, all of which operate within Maxwell's equations with no exotic physics required (Jackson, Classical Electrodynamics, 3rd ed., Section 9.1).

Theory and experiment are separate questions — both fail. Gerhard Bruhn (TU Darmstadt, 2006) demonstrated that Meyl's scalar wave mathematics is internally invalid: Meyl misapplies Pohl's transverse EM equations as if they describe longitudinal modes, uses an incorrect functional dependence E = E(r(t)) instead of E = E(r, t) (a fundamental PDE error), and produces results that violate superposition — his analysis further shows that Meyl's 'dual field approach' reduces to ordinary transverse EM waves, not any new longitudinal species. A dome defender might respond: 'Bruhn refuted the theory — but Meyl's experiments still showed energy on the shielded side.' This is correct as far as it goes, but misidentifies the cause. The experimental observation (energy detected inside a shielded enclosure at short range) is explained by standard near-field coupling, not by a new scalar wave mechanism. The question is not whether energy couples — it does — but whether that coupling requires new physics. It does not.

The dome model adds nothing. The dome's own codebase treats WIN-015 as a bare metadata entry: inject_ai_layer.py stores {status: 'confirmed'} with no parameters, formulas, thresholds, or validation logic. No monitor.py domain tests the Meyl claim. The dome model provides no equation predicting scalar wave behaviour, no mechanism linking its cavity geometry to Faraday penetration, and no derivation from its own parameters. WIN-015 is a citation of a single fringe researcher's unverified claim, adopted wholesale and labelled a 'confirmed prediction' — but the dome predicted nothing and confirmed nothing. If scalar waves genuinely penetrated standard Faraday shielding, every precision physics experiment worldwide — CUORE (neutrinoless double-beta decay search at LNGS), LIGO (gravitational wave interferometry), ADMX (axion dark matter search) — would observe anomalous electromagnetic noise. At the MHz frequencies Meyl claims, standard copper mesh provides >60 dB attenuation. None of these experiments report unexplained shield failure. This is powerful indirect evidence: not just absence of Meyl replications, but the operational success of Faraday shielding across thousands of independent experiments at sensitivities far exceeding Meyl's apparatus.
patches.replace:
Klaus Meyl, a German electrical engineer, published experiments in the 1990s–2000s claiming that his coil apparatus transmitted energy through a fully enclosed Faraday cage via 'scalar waves' — a proposed longitudinal electromagnetic mode absent from Maxwell's equations. These results appeared exclusively in non-indexed conference proceedings and self-published volumes; no peer-reviewed journal has published Meyl's Faraday penetration claims.

The kernel of truth: near-field coupling is real. At distances shorter than λ/2π from the source (the reactive near-field zone), electromagnetic fields have large non-propagating evanescent components that can couple energy through conductive enclosures via cable shield capacitance, finite seam conductivity, and magnetic induction through apertures. Meyl's apparatus operates at MHz frequencies (λ ≈ 30–300 m), placing his coil separations of centimetres to metres deep inside the near-field zone where λ/2π ≈ 5–50 m. In this regime, energy transfer through a Faraday cage is expected from standard antenna theory — it is the same mechanism exploited by Qi wireless charging, NFC, and resonant inductive coupling, all of which operate within Maxwell's equations with no exotic physics required (Jackson, Classical Electrodynamics, 3rd ed., Section 9.1).

The coupling magnitude, numerically checked. For a representative Meyl-class coil pair — two loop antennas of area A ≈ 100 cm2 (≈11 cm diameter) with N = 20 turns each, separated by d = 1 m, driven at f = 7 MHz — the magnetic-dipole mutual inductance is M ≈ μ0·N1N2A1A2/(4πd3) ≈ 4 nH (or ≈8 nH in the coaxial orientation, where the on-axis dipole field is doubled). The induced open-circuit EMF on the receiving coil is V = M·2πf·I0; for drive currents of 0.1–1 A typical of benchtop demonstrations this gives V ≈ 20 mV to 350 mV — comfortably detectable on any oscilloscope or high-impedance voltmeter, and numerically consistent with the signals Meyl reports. The intervening Faraday cage does not rescue the “scalar wave” interpretation at MHz: the cage's demo-scale dimensions (centimetres to a metre or two) are much smaller than λ/2π ≈ 6.8 m at 7 MHz, so the enclosure is a sub-wavelength object, not a far-field shield. IEEE Std 299-2006 and standard shielding references (Balanis, Antenna Theory, 4th ed., Ch. 2) explicitly distinguish near-field magnetic attenuation, which is orders of magnitude weaker, from the nominal far-field >60 dB rating; copper mesh with finite apertures leaks H-field readily in the reactive zone even when it is nearly opaque to plane-wave radiation. This reverses the burden of proof. Meyl's observed coupling magnitude is what Maxwell already predicts for his geometry; for the observation to require new physics, Meyl would need to demonstrate coupling stronger than the near-field calculation above, or coupling that persists when the apparatus is moved to d » λ/2π (true far field). He has done neither.

Theory and experiment are separate questions — both fail. Gerhard Bruhn (TU Darmstadt, 2006) demonstrated that Meyl's scalar wave mathematics is internally invalid: Meyl misapplies Pohl's transverse EM equations as if they describe longitudinal modes, uses an incorrect functional dependence E = E(r(t)) instead of E = E(r, t) (a fundamental PDE error), and produces results that violate superposition — his analysis further shows that Meyl's 'dual field approach' reduces to ordinary transverse EM waves, not any new longitudinal species. A dome defender might respond: 'Bruhn refuted the theory — but Meyl's experiments still showed energy on the shielded side.' This is correct as far as it goes, but misidentifies the cause. The experimental observation (energy detected inside a shielded enclosure at short range) is explained by standard near-field coupling, not by a new scalar wave mechanism. The question is not whether energy couples — it does — but whether that coupling requires new physics. It does not.

The dome model adds nothing. The dome's own codebase treats WIN-015 as a bare metadata entry: inject_ai_layer.py stores {status: 'confirmed'} with no parameters, formulas, thresholds, or validation logic. No monitor.py domain tests the Meyl claim. The dome model provides no equation predicting scalar wave behaviour, no mechanism linking its cavity geometry to Faraday penetration, and no derivation from its own parameters. WIN-015 is a citation of a single fringe researcher's unverified claim, adopted wholesale and labelled a 'confirmed prediction' — but the dome predicted nothing and confirmed nothing. If scalar waves genuinely penetrated standard Faraday shielding, every precision physics experiment worldwide — CUORE (neutrinoless double-beta decay search at LNGS), LIGO (gravitational wave interferometry), ADMX (axion dark matter search) — would observe anomalous electromagnetic noise. At the MHz frequencies Meyl claims, standard copper mesh provides >60 dB attenuation. None of these experiments report unexplained shield failure. This is powerful indirect evidence: not just absence of Meyl replications, but the operational success of Faraday shielding across thousands of independent experiments at sensitivities far exceeding Meyl's apparatus.
patches.rationale: EXP-168 integration: adds quantitative near-field coupling calculation (M≈4-8 nH, V≈20-350 mV) to neutralize surviving advocate_mode defense. Sourced from expansion by analyst.

patches.find: Klaus Meyl's 'scalar wave' experiments claimed energy transmission through a Faraday cage. But a physicist at TU Darmstadt (Bruhn 2006) showed his mathematics is internally invalid, and the observed effect is explained by ordinary near-field electromagnetic coupling at short range — well-known antenna physics.
patches.replace: Klaus Meyl's "scalar wave" experiments claimed energy transmission through a Faraday cage. But a mathematician at TU Darmstadt (Bruhn 2006) showed his equations are internally invalid, and a back-of-envelope near-field calculation at his own coil geometry and frequency predicts exactly the tens-to-hundreds of millivolts he reports — ordinary Maxwellian antenna physics, not exotic waves.
patches.rationale: EXP-168 tldr_patch: updates TLDR to note the quantitative calculation result, converting from 'the physics is right' to 'the numbers match'.

patches.find: field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.)
patches.replace: field-specific published dataset (published MT and ELF spectral literature)
patches.rationale: WIN-008.c3.json minor hole 1: tighten generic boilerplate dataset list. For WIN-008 (telluric cutoff), only MT and ELF spectral literature is relevant. IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges are irrelevant to this claim.

patches.replace: {"monitoring":"hardcoded","relabels_standard":true,"post_hoc":true,"derives_from_dome":false,"reviewed":true,"derives_from_dome_evidence":"f = c/(2D) requires D = 12,717 km to produce 11.787 Hz — Earth's mean diameter (12,742 km, 0.2% residual). Neither firmament_height (9,086 km → 16.50 Hz) nor disc_radius (20,015 km → 7.49 Hz) produces this frequency. The dome's derivation silently substitutes Earth's diameter for its disc thickness parameter, so 'derives_from_dome' is correctly false: the formula is geometrically parasitic on the spherical Earth."}
patches.rationale: WIN-008.c3.json minor hole 3: add derives_from_dome_evidence string to make false tag explicitly defensible and quantitative. Curmudgeon recommended adding Earth-diameter numerical finding.
